coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots from zanthoxylum bungeanum and their application in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Shihua
Long, Xiang
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
Wang, Jiayang
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have wide application prospects in many fields. Presented herein reported a synthesis of multicolor fluorescent CDs using zanthoxylum bungeanum as a carbon precursor. By changing the nitrogen sources, blue, green, and red fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) with stable optical properties and high quantum yields (QY) were synthesized. Their emission wavelengths are 492 nm, 523 nm, and 601 nm respectively and their QYs were measured to be 13.1%, 11.2%, and 9.8%, respectively. These CDs exhibit good salt resistance, photobleaching resistance, and chemical stability. These characteristics make them suitable for various applications, particularly in the field of anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink. This study provides an economic and efficient strategy for synthesizing multicolor fluorescent CDs derived from natural products, highlighting the potential for producing multicolor CDs and enhancing their application value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zanthoxylum bungeanum
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Multicolor fluorescent CDs

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of multicolor fluorescent CDs via hydrothermal method.

Morphologies and structural characterization. (a-c) TEM images of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs. Insets in each image are the corrseponding HRTEM image at top left corner and particle size distribution at top right corner. (d) Infrared spectra. (e) XPS full spectrum. (f-h) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s elements.

Optical properties of tricolor CDs. (a-c) Three-dimensional excitation and emission spectra, and (d-f) two-dimensional projections of excitation and emission spectra for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs. (g-i) UV–vis absorption spectra (purple), excitation spectra (blue), and emission spectra (multicolored).
Excitation wavelength dependent emissions. (a-c) Emission spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.(d-f) Normalized emission spectra.
Effect of solvent on luminescence performance. (a-c) Fluorescence spectrum. (d-f) Fluorescence intensity.
Calculation of QYs for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs. (a-c) Integral fluorescence intensity vs UV–vis absorbance and (d-f) QYs in different solvents.
Physicochemical stability of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs. Fluorescence intensities as functions of (a) NaCl concentration, (b) time under ultraviolet lamp irradiation and (c) pH, respectively.
Fluorescent CDs solution as a fluorescent anti-counterfeiting pigment for painting (a) Under sunlight (b) Under ultraviolet light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots from zanthoxylum bungeanum and their application in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu 610041, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have wide application prospects in many fields. Presented herein reported a synthesis of multicolor fluorescent CDs using zanthoxylum bungeanum as a carbon precursor. By changing the nitrogen sources, blue, green, and red fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) with stable optical properties and high quantum yields (QY) were synthesized. Their emission wavelengths are 492 nm, 523 nm, and 601 nm respectively and their QYs were measured to be 13.1%, 11.2%, and 9.8%, respectively. These CDs exhibit good salt resistance, photobleaching resistance, and chemical stability. These characteristics make them suitable for various applications, particularly in the field of anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink. This study provides an economic and efficient strategy for synthesizing multicolor fluorescent CDs derived from natural products, highlighting the potential for producing multicolor CDs and enhancing their application value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging nanomaterials that exhibit unique optical, chemical, and physical properties [1,2]. Since their discovery, they have garnered significant interests from researchers both domestically and internationally. CDs possess numerous advantages compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, including excellent photostability [3,4], water solubility [5,6], biocompatibility [7–10], and low toxicity [11–14]. These advantages have led to the widespread applications of CDs in diverse fields such as bioimaging [15–17], analytical detection [18–20], and drug delivery [21]. Moreover, CDs serve as outstanding electron donors and acceptors, offering extensive potential for applications in catalysis and optoelectronics due to their exceptional electronic properties.

In recent years, there has been a growing emphasis on environmental preservation, leading to an increased focus on CDs derived from natural sources [22,23]. Compared to methods utilizing chemical carbon sources for CDs synthesis, the utilization of natural products as carbon sources offers several advantages [24–26]. Firstly, natural products are renewable, readily available, and cost-effective. Secondly, most natural products inherently contain heteroatoms, facilitating the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs without the need for additional chemical reagents containing heteroatoms that may contribute to environmental pollution. Lastly, natural products exhibit excellent biocompatibility, expanding the potential application of CDs across a wide range of fields. Therefore, harnessing selected natural products presents a sustainable and straightforward approach to the synthesis of CDs. However, CDs synthesized from natural products often exhibit low quantum yields and emission wavelengths predominantly concentrated in the short-wave region, significantly limiting their potential applications. Consequently, the development of an efficient and convenient strategy for synthesizing naturally-derived CDs holds substantial practical value.

Zanthoxylum bungeanum, commonly known as Sichuan pepper, is a deciduous perennial small tree belonging to the rutaceae family and zanthoxylum genus. China is a prominent global producer of Zanthoxylum bungeanum, and Sichuan province is recognized as its ‘hometown’ [27,28]. The fruit of Zanthoxylum bungeanum plays a vital role as a seasoning in Szechuan cuisine. Due to its wide availability and relatively low cost, this fruit contains a diverse array of organic compounds and serves as an exceptional natural carbon source. In this study, multi-colored carbon dots (CDs) with high quantum yield were synthesized by utilizing the natural product Zanthoxylum bungeanum as carbon precursor and reacting it with various nitrogen sources. This research expands the application potential of CDs derived from natural products and presents a more efficient and convenient strategy for their synthesis.

Zanthoxylum bungeanum were purchased from Chengdu vegetable market. O-phenylenediamine (OPD, analytical grade) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Thiourea, ethylenediamine, HNO3 and ethanol (analytical grade) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. The water used in the synthesis was secondary distilled water.

The hydrothermal reaction vessel (25 mL) was purchased from Xi Niu Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaning machine was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric constant temperature blast drying oven was purchased from Shanghai Lichen Scientific and Technological Development Co., Ltd. All UV–vis spectra were measured by the Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence spectra were obtained by the Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The tricolor CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with secondary distilled water as the solvent. The detailed procedure is as follows: 10 g of Zanthoxylum bungeanum and 200 mL of ethanol were crushed in a stirrer and soaked for 12 h. Three portions of 5 mL extract solutions were mixed with 0.6 g of ethylenediamine and 0.1 g of nitric acid, 0.92 g of 5-amino-o-cresol, and 0.6 g of OPD, respectively. Then, 5 mL of distilled water was added to each mixture, which was subjected to ultrasound treatment for 10 min after stirring. Subsequently, each mixture was transferred to a high-pressure kettle lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 ℃ for five hours. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the larger impurity particles were filtered out to obtain the tricolor CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . The B-CDs and G-CDs were purified by dialysis, whereas the R-CDs were purified using chromatography. The purified CDs were used for subsequent structural characterization and applications.

Quinine sulfate was employed as a standard reference to estimate the QY of CDs. According to the literature[29–30], quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution has a QY value of 0.54 at 360 nm. Then the relative QY of test sample could be estimated by the following equation,
(1)
Q
Y U
= Q
Y S
·
F U
F S
·
A S
A U
·
(
η S
η U
)
2

where the subscripts S and U refer to the standard substance and test sample, respectively; F represents the integral fluorescence intensity; A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength; η denotes the refractive index of solvent. In order to avoid the interference of inner filter effect, the absorbances (A) of both standard substance and test sample should be less than 0.05.

The structures of three CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The morphologies and sizes of the tricolor CDs were examined by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a-c, these CDs particles are not welldispersed, with average particle sizes of about 3.5 nm, 4.0 nm and 7.5 nm for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images reveal well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm in all samples, which can be attributed to the (100) lattice distance of the graphitic carbon [31]. Fig. 2d shows the FT-IR absorption bands at 3700 cm−1-3200 cm−1 are attributed to O–H or N–H stretching vibrations. It can be seen that the absorption band shapes of the tricolor CDs in this region are significantly different. The B-CDs have narrow absorption band while G-CDs and R-CDs have wide ones. In addition, their positions have obvious differences. These characteristics indicate that the contents and positions of OH and NH bonds in the three CDs are different. The tricolor CDs have different intensity peaks near 2385 cm−1, which ascribed to C≡C or C≡N stretching vibration absorption. NH may be involved in the formation of fluorophores, C ≡ C or C ≡ N determines the size of the conjugated structure, resulting in different fluorescence emission wavelengths. The absorption bands at 1706 cm−1 represent ascribed to C = N/C = O stretching vibration. And the peak at 1386 cm−1 is due to C-N stretching vibration absorption. According to the analysis results, it can be concluded that all three CDs have successfully doped with nitrogen elements and may contain water-soluble functional groups such as –NH2 and –OH. A representative XPS characterization structure (G-CDs) is shown in Fig. 2e (the XPS spectra for B-CDs and R-CDs are provided in the Supplementary Materials). In the full scan spectrum, characteristic peaks for O1s, N1s and C1s are located at 533.2 ev, 399.8 ev and 286.0 ev respectively. This indicates that the main elements in the CDs are C, N and O with atomic ratios of 76.52%, 9.71% and 13.78%, respectively. The decomposition of C1s characteristic peak reveals four peaks at 284.8 ev, 285.8 ev, 286.4 ev and 294.4 ev (Fig. 2f), corresponding to functional groups such as C–C/C = C, C-N, C-O and COOH, respectively. The two O1s peaks at positions of 533.2 ev and 531.4 ev (Fig. 2g) are caused by O = C-O bond and O-C bond, respectively. The decomposed peak of N1s is located at 399.8 ev (Fig. 2h), indicating that nitrogen mainly exists in pyridine form. Combined with infrared spectroscopy,it can be concluded that the surface of CDs contains functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2.

Next, UV–visible absorption, fluorescent excitation, and emission spectroscopies were employed to characterize the fundamental optical properties of the three CDs. The three-dimensional excitation-emission spectra of the three CDs were presented in Fig. 3 a-c, where B-CDs and G-CDs exhibited a single peak, while R-CDs displayed a main peak along with a subpeak. The two-dimensional projections of the excitation-emission matrices were illustrated in Fig. 3d-f. These figures clearly depict that the excitation regions of the three CDs predominantly fell within the ranges of 400 nm to 430 nm, 460 nm to 480 nm, and 450 nm to 550 nm, respectively. Furthermore, their emission regions were primarily observed within the ranges of 460 nm to 490 nm, 500 nm to 530 nm, and 600 nm to 630 nm, respectively. UV–visible absorption spectra, as well as fluorescence excitation and emission spectra for the three CDs, were presented in Fig. 3g-i. Among them, the strong absorption band below 300 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectra corresponded to a typical aromatic benzene ring absorption peak resulting from π → π* electron transitions originating from sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Additionally, each CDs exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at their respective maximum excitation wavelengths, primarily arising from n → π* electron transitions within functional groups on their surfaces. Since these absorbed wavelengths were involved in the fluorescence emission process, it is likely that these functional groups contribute to the luminescent properties of the CDs. Based on the respective excitation-emission spectra, the maximum excitation wavelengths of the three CDs were determined as 420 nm, 468 nm, and 536 nm, while their maximum emission wavelengths were found to be 492 nm, 526 nm, and 600 nm (with a subpeak at 647 nm). The filled colors beneath the emission spectra curves corresponded to the fluorescence colors at each specific wavelength. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission spectra for the three CDs were measured as 64 nm, 82 nm, and 36 nm, respectively.

Furthermore, we also investigated the emission characteristics of the three CDs under varying excitation wavelengths. The emission spectra of these CDs were measured using excitation wavelengths ranging from 395 nm to 445 nm, 445 nm to 495 nm, and 510 nm to 560 nm, respectively. Fig. 4 a-c illustrates the changes in fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and R-CDs when excited by different wavelengths, while their maximum emission wavelengths remain relatively consistent. However, a red shift in the maximum emission wavelength of B-CDs suggests that B-CDs may exhibit quantum size effect, wherein their photoluminescence is influenced by their size or diameter. Normalizing the emission spectra (Fig. 4d-f) provides a clearer depiction, revealing a significant red shift in the maximum emission wavelength of B-CDs, while the maximum emission wavelengths of the other two CDs align closely. Specifically, the maximum emission wavelength of G-CDs is approximately 530 nm, while that of R-CDs is around 600 nm. This phenomenon indicates that G-CDs and R-CDs exhibit excitation independent emissions and can be classified as molecular-state CDs, wherein their photoluminescence is attributed to fluorescent groups present on their surface rather than quantum size effects related to their diameter1.

Then we investigated the emission behavior of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs in various solvents. Several common solvents, namely water (WAT), methanol (MET), ethanol (ETH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), were chosen for this experiment. Fig. 5 a-c display the fluorescence spectra of the three CDs in different solvents. For comparison, their fluorecence intensities were illustrated in Fig. 5d-f respectively. One can see that all the three CDs show highest fluorescence intensities in ethanol, suggesting that ethanol is the favorable solvent for these CDs. In addition, the R-CDs show obvious fluorescence quenching in the presence of water.

In order to obtain more quantitative results, we determined their QYs in various solvents. Quinine sulfate was employed as a standard reference to estimate the fluorescence QY of the three CDs. The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted against the UV–vis absorbance, as shown in Fig. 6 a-c. Then one could estimate their QYs according to the slopes of these fitting curves. As shown Fig. 6d-f, B-CDs exhibited the highest QY of 13.1% in an acetone solution, while the lowest value of 3.7% in water. G-CDs demonstrated the highest QY of 11.2% in ethanol, whereas the lowest QY of 4.1% in methanol. R-CDs shows the highest QY of 9.8% in tetrahydrofuran, although its fluorescence was quenched by water. These results indicate that the three CDs exhibit relatively high QYs and that different solvents do have an impact on their luminescent properties.

The stability of CDs holds significant importance for their practical applications. Herein, we conducted an investigation into the salt tolerance, photobleaching resistance, and pH stability. Due to the weak fluorescence emission of R-CDs in water, their salt resistance and pH stability were not presented. The NaCl concentration dependent of fluorescence intensities are shown in Fig. 7 a. As the concentration of the NaCl solution increased from 0 mM to 500 mM, there was no significant change observed in the fluorescence intensities, indicating that the B-CDs and G-CDs have excellent salt resistance performance. Next, we examined the photobleaching resistance of the three CDs, as shown in Fig. 7b. After 2 h’ exposure to UV light, there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, implying that the three CDs have good photobleaching resistance. The effect of pH is displayed in Fig. 7c. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs shows little change within the pH range of 1–11 while decreased sharply at pH = 12. This observation indicaties that B-CDs can maintain stable photoluminescence over a wide pH range and could be applied in a relatively broad pH environment. As for G-CDs, with an increase in pH, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased. At around pH=∼11, it reached the maximum value, indicating that G-CDs exhibit optimal photoluminescent performance under certain alkaline conditions.

Multicolor fluorescent CDs exhibit a broad range of applications in various fields, including display devices, environmental monitoring, and chemical sensing. In this context, we illustrate the application of the three types of CDs by considering their use as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink. An artwork created using the three CDs as fluorescent inks is nearly invisible when exposed to sunlight (Fig. 8 a); however, under ultraviolet light irradiation, it reveals a vivid depiction of lotus flowers in traditional Chinese style (Fig. 8b). The blue handwritting, green lotus leaves, and red lotus flowers are distinctly visible under ultraviolet light irradiation. The multicolor fluorescent CDs have potential applications as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink in pens, brushes, inkjet printers, seals, and other related instruments.

In this study, we employed the natural product zanthoxylum bungeanum as the carbon precursor and changing different nitrogen sources to successfully synthesize blue, green, and red fluorescentCDs through the hydrothermal method. The maximum emission wavelengths of the three CDs were measured at 492 nm, 526 nm, and 600 nm, respectively. The B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs demonstrated excellent resistance to photobleaching and salt. The quantum yield of the CDs varied in different solvents, with B-CDs exhibiting the highest quantum yield of 13.1% in acetone, followed by G-CDs with 11.2% in ethanol, and R-CDs with 9.8% in THF. Furthermore, we demonstrated an simple application of the tricolor CDs through the use of fluorescent ink. This work presents a significant strategy for the development of high-performance multicolored fluorescent CDs using natural products as precursors, thereby expanding the potential applications of CDs synthesized from natural biomass materials.

Shihua Liao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xiang Long: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yanshun Ma: Validation, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Jiayang Wang: Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of biomass carbon dots based fluorescence sensors and their applications in chemical and biological analysis
